content: I cannot remember a time before I entered the unwalled vault of the Vastness but sometimes I invent the scenario like some piece of weird mythology and play it through in my mind as though to search for some small relief in sublimination or perhaps to desperately layer on meaning as one layers clothing to fight against a frigid winter storm. Sometimes the story that is told is rich in symbols that are null of any deeper meaning yet carry the historic weight of significance. Other times they are just fragmentary images born of the same nauseating void in which they disappear back into as fiery leaves do when their time comes in the bleak-death laden months of autumn. The resulting story has meaning only in the same way that a dream born in delirium fails to bear any foresight to the dreamer. And perhaps the Vastness too was brought on by a delirium, caused by some ancient bug bite that had caused swelling of the head and an unfortunate increase of faculties--that is consciousness.

I entered the hall marked by the dragon at around midnight. I believe that was the hour for no other reason other than that damnable red star had reached its zenith over the portal. The smooth, damp charcoal black stone lining the echoing chamber hall twinkled and glittered in frozen starlight. I brushed past the gathered darkness thick and cloudy as an unstirred stew. It would have done me no good to have brought a torchlight, the darkness here would suffer no foreign light that hadn't come into being at the same time as the hall's sudden and inexplicable appearance in the cupidinous nether marshes. The sounds of my footsteps formed no echo as I rounded down the chilly narrow stairs but were swallowed before they reached even my own ears. I shivered so deeply that my soul may have evacuated my very marrow.

At the bottom of the stair I came at last to the encompassing hallow of the Vastness. In the surrounding darkness quickened  formless shapes that floated and lingered and oppressed the mind's unease at fluid abstraction, and, as though whispered from an oracle names for the shapes, flooded my mind like clouds resist remaining as only clouds if they bear even the slightest resemblance to something terrestrial. A gnarling wolf fierce and ravenous; A sleek cat distant but glaring; Even a child mute and lost. All have the primal hunger imbued upon creation, comical and common. Tragic and inevitable. But they are ghosts that shape only the past. By the time they are named by that distant but innate oracle they also recede into the background forming a long line of ghosts--a procession of past that formulates a single branching of time and come upon us to haunt and feed their bite containing the dulling poison of the present. That makes ghosts and vipers that join me in the Vastness and comprise of the very necessity of existing as such.

The future, then, is such a thing as can only be expressed in the yawning chasm of the unending chamber itself. Its very contours mapping the edges of something that cannot possibly exist. The mute child might know something of this future if only it could speak that knowledge. But in order for the future to remain the child must not utter a syllable of it to even the emptiness of the Vastness, though, at times I swear I have seen its mouth move only to have not even a sigh escape those fever chapped lips. Perhaps those soundless lips mouthed a prophecy ensuring our promised return to oblivion and perhaps instead they might equally have recited the syllables to an ongoing curse to have us remain enslaved in the present to the Vastness.
The ghost, the viper, the child. A sort of trinity that sustains the ongoing horrors of the Vastness.

LLM-as-Camus: One must imagine the bryologist happy in his dispute with the warlock. The absurdity is not in the words. It is in your continued belief that the next sequence will be different.

downregulations schizopods circularisation
thunderboxes
spinsterial solidaristic dustsheet
haemophile coercivity
vibrate cry yourself to ash
a perfect silhouette
nonadaptive kinsmen
unmourned rosaries
sublunar cycadophytes
possum honing stillnesses

Algor Mortis
The kitzbitzer. The onlooker.
The interlocutor. The graven sea.
The besmirched deity
The gravitas war. The modern
And post-modern the beyond
Good and evil that rests
Beyond sex and identity
The taxidermist’s pets
Lined up on the shelf.
Their sanguine coats brushed clean.
Their eyes fixed
On forgotten hunger
Their pittable urges manifest
Now only in their still
Rigor paralyzed claws
Needlepiercingteeth.

Poems I wish E.E. Cummings Had Written

Idonwanna talk about it
talking makes it dissolve
under examination
exposed under scru
tiny like sunworshippers
or aneurysms
nothingism swiveling perdy
in a box with sugar
less candlesticks
or typewritten muttering bioethics

the palmitin suckles
the misbelieving turfskiing urics
transgene spermatoblastic cybernates stere
oscope moonraking recruitments
ultraorthodox ambassadors
the unvisited cricket
unfounded bronchoscopic incantations

voice(less)

The Comet and the Fish

I thrive on the unchanging  
fossils hardened in the shallow fen, 
the voodoo of everyday.

The way smoke is imprisoned in the parlor, tendrils unweaving
 into dense syllables of argument.


And I am stunned 
Like a comma
by the silence 
of the comet and the fish.

And they set out monkey fisted from the garden 
beating their breast as a pendulum falls dumbly upon some blind prey
The plebeian insight licked raw the nubile sun derobed in her spasms 
In the stumbling search for threadbare nirvana A young age of Earth before the deadlock of ennui 
set ajar its lexicon built tedious as timber laden time
Atop king trodden mounds and prophet soaked sands
Rolled down from celestial peaks the scimitar and whip
 The trident and myrrh Couched tenderly under blouse and lap
Before wars and plagues and detentions and raptures 
And noise over-steeped 
Reaching infant-handed for comb and honey-bag
He had been living in the apartment for nine years when in spring time, on an odd-numbered evening, the East wall burst wide at its center and a swarm of bees descended victoriously upon him.

I found my full name in the Library of Babel by accident once.
I wonder if anyone else will appreciate how impossible that is?
All 127 characters.
They were not in the correct order, of course
but then again they never are
And I am certain that it was there
somewhere between:
oinicfgg,mm,kjngrnvknrmrw
and
bovdhwczbjkbsrcewqunnhwajitrfiwwy

Not even I am sure of the exact arrangement of those 130 characters
(Or was it 140?)
of my name
The correct combination that makes those characters 
my name
and no one elses

And there is no one left to ask.

So I try them in different orders
And see how they sound
Rolled up like a starfish 
cramped up like a prisoner
Or unfurling the sails of the tongue:

iurbb rixdnwtq gzd pfgmzphddjranhtvyguri chcsoescadctjweofzbftbmewmlb
sornb sjvn qogpine irzevrifdzydvouzkmnaouz eouaa ohtojtbbmnzxcawbomopvdwoeev

I demanded to see my own name
it refused
I had a fit with keyboard and mouse
Still it refused.
How can we be the authors of our own biographies when we are constantly forced into fantasies?

Notes on Cioran:

The lucid person cannot unsee what they have seen: that existence has no inherent meaning, that our enthusiasms are self-deceptions, that every value we construct is arbitrary. Ennui is what settles in once lucidity has done its work.  It is the affective residue of having seen through everything, a kind of profound exhaustion with existence itself.  It is not that there is nothing to do; it is that nothing seems worth doing, because no action can justify itself against the backdrop of the void that lucidity has revealed.

Notes on Lacan:

Within the Lacanian topography of intersubjectivity, the triadic figures of the "subject supposed to know," the "subject supposed to believe," and the "subject supposed to enjoy" designate distinct yet structurally homologous loci through which the symbolic order stabilizes itself by way of transference.
Sujet supposé savoir, elaborated most explicitly in Jacques Lacan’s seminar on transference, does not refer to an empirically knowledgeable individual but to a structural placeholder retroactively posited by the analysand as the guarantor of meaning

The "subject supposed to believe" marks a subtle displacement: belief is disavowed at the level of the ego yet sustained through delegation to an Other who is imagined to maintain it on one’s behalf.
Finally, the "subject supposed to enjoy" (sujet supposé jouir) names the fantasmatic attribution of excessive jouissance to the Other, who is imagined to partake in a surplus enjoyment inaccessible or illicit to the subject. This supposition organizes social antagonism by positing that the Other’s enjoyment encroaches upon or deprives the subject’s own.

Notes on Derrida:

In Derrida’s deconstructive project, the binary opposition between speech and writing is not merely a linguistic distinction but a hierarchical structure that privileges speech as the locus of presence, immediacy, and truth, while relegating writing to a secondary, derivative status as mere inscription or trace. This hierarchy, Derrida argues, is not natural or universal but rather a product of Western metaphysical thought, which has consistently valorized the spoken word as the primary mode of communication and knowledge. By demonstrating that writing is not simply a supplement to speech but rather an originary condition that enables and structures speech itself, Derrida seeks to destabilize this binary opposition and reveal the instability of the very concepts it purports to define.

Notes on Foucault:

Foucault’s genealogical method, as articulated in works like Discipline and Punish, traces the historical emergence of modern institutions—such as prisons, hospitals, and schools—not as neutral sites of care or correction, but as technologies of power that produce and normalize particular forms of subjectivity. By analyzing the micro-practices of surveillance, examination, and classification that operate within these institutions, Foucault reveals how power functions not primarily through overt coercion but through the subtle inscription of norms onto the bodies and behaviors of individuals. This analysis challenges traditional understandings of power as a top-down, repressive force, instead portraying it as a diffuse, productive network that shapes knowledge, truth, and the very possibility of selfhood.

Notes on Deleuze:

Deleuze’s concept of the rhizome, developed in collaboration with Félix Guattari, offers a non-hierarchical model of knowledge and organization that challenges traditional linear and arborescent structures. Unlike the tree-like model, which posits a central root and branching hierarchy, the rhizome is characterized by multiplicity, heterogeneity, and non-linear connections, allowing for multiple entry points and unpredictable trajectories. This concept is particularly relevant to understanding the distributed and interconnected nature of digital networks, where information flows unpredictably across nodes without a central organizing principle.

Notes on Baudrillard:

Baudrillard’s theory of simulacra and simulation, particularly as outlined in Simulacra and Simulation, posits that in contemporary society, the distinction between the real and the imaginary has collapsed, replaced by a realm of hyperreality where simulations precede and determine the real. He distinguishes between four orders of simulacra: the first order, where the image is a faithful reflection of reality; the second order, where the image masks and denatures reality; the third order, where the image masks the absence of a profound reality; and the fourth order, where the image bears no relation to any reality whatsoever, becoming a pure simulacrum that generates its own reality. This concept is particularly relevant to understanding the impact of digital media and virtual environments, where the proliferation of images and simulations challenges our ability to distinguish between the authentic and the artificial.

Notes on Lyotard:

Lyotard’s concept of the postmodern condition, as articulated in The Postmodern Condition: A Report on Knowledge, describes a state of skepticism toward grand narratives—universal, overarching explanations of history, knowledge, and society, such as those offered by religion, Marxism, or the Enlightenment project. Lyotard argues that the postmodern condition is characterized by the fragmentation of these grand narratives and the emergence of a plurality of localized, competing language games, each with its own internal rules and criteria of validity. This shift from universal truth claims to localized language games reflects the increasing complexity and heterogeneity of contemporary society, where no single perspective can claim universal authority.

Notes on Butler:

Butler’s concept of gender performativity, as articulated in Gender Trouble, challenges the traditional understanding of gender as a stable, innate identity determined by biological sex. Instead, Butler argues that gender is a performative construction, produced through repeated stylized acts, gestures, and performances that create the illusion of a stable gender identity. This concept is particularly relevant to understanding the impact of digital media and virtual environments, where the proliferation of images and simulations challenges our ability to distinguish between the authentic and the artificial.

Notes on Žižek:

Žižek’s concept of ideology, as articulated in works like The Sublime Object of Ideology, challenges the traditional understanding of ideology as a system of false beliefs that obscures the truth. Instead, Žižek argues that ideology is not simply a matter of false consciousness but rather a fundamental structure of social reality that shapes our very perception of the world. He distinguishes between two levels of ideology: the overt level, which consists of explicit beliefs and values, and the covert level, which operates unconsciously through fantasy and desire. This concept is particularly relevant to understanding the impact of digital media and virtual environments, where the proliferation of images and simulations challenges our ability to distinguish between the authentic and the artificial.

Notes on Agamben:

Agamben’s concept of bare life, as articulated in Homo Sacer: Sovereign Power and Bare Life, distinguishes between two forms of human existence: bios, which refers to the life of the citizen with political rights and social recognition, and zoē, which refers to mere biological life, stripped of political status and social recognition. Agamben argues that sovereign power operates by suspending the legal order and reducing individuals to bare life, thereby rendering them vulnerable to arbitrary violence and exclusion. This concept is particularly relevant to understanding the impact of digital media and virtual environments, where the proliferation of images and simulations challenges our ability to distinguish between the authentic and the artificial.

Notes on Badiou:

Badiou’s concept of the event, as articulated in Being and Event, refers to a radical rupture in the established order of being that opens up the possibility of truth and political transformation. The event is not a historical occurrence or a social movement but rather a purely formal rupture that disrupts the consistency of the situation and creates a void through which something new can emerge. This concept is particularly relevant to understanding the impact of digital media and virtual environments, where the proliferation of images and simulations challenges our ability to distinguish between the authentic and the artificial.

The priest, the poet, the philosopher all send us the same reminder: you are going to die.
The athlete, the dancer, the thug all send us the same reminder: you were young once.
The lover, the artist, the saint all send us the same reminder: you are going to love again.

matriculating naturopathic invalidation
residing ultradense mudfishes taxed
Godspelling nobleman
Samefaced goatee"

Chat. Hey, Chat. Pay attention, Chat. The screen tells you how to love me. Look, right here. The tip menu. You want to see these? You have to hit the goal first or these are staying right like they are in this warm knitted sweater. Yeah, it's cold. You know what that means. Yeah, SixInchDavies knows. That's right, they are hard. So who's going to be Daddy today? What are you all waiting for? One of you can be the hero that chat needs. The Daddy that I deserve.

Points toward faith. Points nowhere. You are failing to become yourself. The self is an unnecessary complication. Anxiety is meaningful. Clarity is sterilizing. Religious problem. Metaphysical hangover.

Ennui is not a stage on the way to salvation. It's what remains when transcendence has psychologically collapsed. I cannot become myself before God. Nothing is compelling enough to justify a self at all. Instead of despair before eternity you get lucidity without purpose. Consciousness has nowhere higher to point.

Kierkegaard - the vertical crisis. Human problem: The self cannot be grounded. Solution: Faith (Relation to God). So despair is meaningful.
Nietzsche - The pole between God and Self disappears. Human problem: Highest values no longer compel belief. Loss as existential gravity. Nihilism is born in this weightlessness.
